div.div: Nothing special — just the game board on page 1.

Assign everyone an instrument to mime and vocalize — violin, trumpet, drums, flute, tuba. The leader starts performing their instrument; everyone copies their own assigned instrument. When the leader switches to a different instrument, everyone must instantly switch to copy the leader's new instrument — abandoning their old one. The trick: the leader can switch rapidly, fake-out, or add gestures to confuse players. Anyone who plays the wrong instrument, switches too slowly, or stops performing must do a silly penalty (tell a joke, sing a line of a song).

The name 'Dutch Concert' was a Victorian expression for a chaotic, cacophonous noise. The game was deliberately designed to produce gloriously awful racket — 'Dutch' being used loosely to mean incomprehensible and foreign.

Each player is assigned a musical instrument to imitate with their voice and body. A leader begins conducting the 'concert'; whenever the leader changes their own instrument imitation, all players must switch to imitating whatever instrument they are now doing. Anyone who fails to follow correctly pays a forfeit.

svg.text: Source: The Sociable, or One Thousand and One Home Amusements (1858) · Heritage Parlor
